answer:
**Yājñavalkya Smṛti chapter 2 (on legal procedure) discusses evidence ("proof") explicitly at verse 2.22, identifying it as a document, possession, or witnesses (with ordeals prescribed if these are absent).[^1]**

**Verses around 2.85 (in the "Documents" subsection) detail how to execute a valid written document—a key form of evidence/proof. Specifically:**

- **Verse 2.84**: When settling a matter by mutual consent, "a document with witnesses should be made, on which the creditor's name is written first."[^2]
- **Verse 2.85**: "It should be marked with the year, month, half-month, day, name, caste, and personal gotra, along with the name of one's fellow-student and one's own father's name."[^3]
- **Verse 2.86**: The debtor writes their own name and affirmation at the end.[^4]

**These requirements ensure the document's authenticity and evidentiary value (valid even without witnesses if self-written, per 2.89; provable via handwriting comparison, etc., per 2.92).[^5] The surrounding context (verses 2.84–94) covers documents' creation, proof, and enforcement in disputes.**

[^1]: Yājñavalkya Smṛti, ch. 2, [p-e6e133](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/#p-e6e133).

[^2]: Yājñavalkya Smṛti, ch. 2, [p-777ef3](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/#p-777ef3).

[^3]: Yājñavalkya Smṛti, ch. 2, [p-86a2c9](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/#p-86a2c9).

[^4]: Yājñavalkya Smṛti, ch. 2, [p-8aace1](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/#p-8aace1).

[^5]: Yājñavalkya Smṛti, ch. 2, [p-4f0f8d](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/#p-4f0f8d) and [p-4514ef](/works/unknown-yajnavalkya-smrti-cb88d6/02/translation/#p-4514ef).
